title: Whether to Save the Iron Bridge
summary: A debate between a structural engineer and a local historian over a derelict nineteenth-century iron footbridge in a Welsh valley — one arguing it must come down, the other that it must be saved, each conceding something the other is right about.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: Let me be blunt before we get sentimental. The Pontfaen footbridge is failing. The wrought-iron tension members have lost perhaps a third of their section to corrosion, and the original ironmaster's records — which I've read — never anticipated the loads it now carries. My job is to certify whether it is safe. It is not. Whatever it means to people, a structure that cannot hold its own weight is not a heritage asset. It is a liability with a nice view.

body.speaker: Historian
body.text: And I won't pretend the corrosion is imaginary. You're the engineer; I defer to you on the metal. But I'd ask you to notice that you've already smuggled in a definition. You said it's not a heritage asset because it can't carry load. That assumes a heritage asset is something you walk across. The bridge's value was never in getting people from one bank to the other. There's a perfectly good road bridge two hundred metres downstream, and there has been for fifty years.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: Then what, precisely, is it for? I'm genuinely asking. If nobody needs to cross it, and it's dangerous, the kindest thing is a controlled demolition before the river does it for us, messily, in a flood.

body.speaker: Historian
body.text: It's for memory, though I know how soft that sounds to someone holding a corrosion report. Pontfaen was the first iron footbridge cast by the Tregaron foundry, and that foundry employed three generations of this valley before it closed. The bridge is the last physical thing they made that's still standing. Pull it down and the foundry becomes a paragraph in a book nobody opens. Leave it up — even fenced off, even uncrossable — and it remains a fact in the landscape that a child can be walked to and told about.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: I find that genuinely moving, and I'm not being sarcastic. But here's the rub. 'Fenced off and uncrossable' is not free. Someone pays to inspect it annually, to keep the public from the rusting deck, to insure against the day a bit of it lands on a kayaker. You're describing a monument and budgeting for a bridge. If the valley wants a monument to the foundry, I can think of a dozen cheaper ones that don't involve suspending two tonnes of failing iron over moving water.

body.speaker: Historian
body.text: That's a fair hit, and I'll concede part of it. A plaque is cheaper. A replica in the museum car park is cheaper. But you're assuming the meaning is transferable — that a plaque carries what the bridge carries. It doesn't. The bridge convinces because it is the actual thing, in the actual place, bearing the actual weather that's been eating it for a century. The rust isn't a flaw in the monument; the rust is the monument. A clean replica would be a lie that photographs well.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: So you'd have us spend public money preserving a hazard precisely because it's decaying authentically. You see the difficulty. There's a version of your argument that justifies preserving anything old, at any cost, on the grounds that its very dilapidation is meaningful. At some point a community has to decide what it can afford to keep, and 'it's the real thing' can't be a blank cheque.

body.speaker: Historian
body.text: No, it can't, and I wouldn't write you one. I'm not arguing for everything old. I'm arguing for the few things that are load-bearing in the other sense — the ones a community's story actually rests on. The test isn't age. It's whether removing the object would erase something that nothing else can hold. By that test most old things fail, and should come down. I just don't think this one does.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: Then we may be closer than the headline suggests. I could probably stabilise it — not for crossing, but as a static object — for a fraction of full restoration, if the council accepts it will never reopen. What I can't do is certify a romance. Give me a monument to engineer and I'll engineer it honestly. Just don't ask me to call it a bridge, because the moment we both stop pretending it's a bridge, half this argument dissolves.

body.speaker: Historian
body.text: I can live with that. Maybe that's the real concession on my side — that saving it means admitting it has stopped being what it was. We keep it by letting it become something else: not a way across the river, but a way back to the people who built it. I'd sign that. Reluctantly, because I liked imagining someone might cross it again. But I'd sign it.

annotations.meaningVi: thẳng thừng, nói toạc
các thanh chịu lực kéo bằng sắt rèn

annotations.phrase: lost perhaps a third of their section to corrosion
annotations.meaningVi: mất có lẽ một phần ba tiết diện do ăn mòn (gỉ sét)
ông chủ xưởng luyện sắt
chứng nhận, xác nhận chính thức
tài sản di sản

annotations.phrase: a liability with a nice view
annotations.meaningVi: một gánh nặng pháp lý kèm theo cảnh đẹp — câu mỉa mai của kỹ sư

annotations.phrase: I defer to you on the metal
annotations.meaningVi: Tôi nhường quyền phán xét cho anh về phần kim loại (tôi tôn trọng chuyên môn của anh)
lén đưa vào một định nghĩa (giả định ngầm chưa được thừa nhận)

annotations.phrase: from one bank to the other
annotations.meaningVi: từ bờ này sang bờ kia (sông)
ở hạ lưu, phía dưới dòng
phá dỡ có kiểm soát
xưởng đúc kim loại

annotations.phrase: a paragraph in a book nobody opens
annotations.meaningVi: một đoạn văn trong cuốn sách không ai mở ra — ẩn dụ cho việc bị lãng quên
một sự thật hiện hữu trong cảnh quan (vật thể thật chứ không phải chữ nghĩa)
vấn đề nằm ở chỗ này (thành ngữ từ Shakespeare)
mặt cầu đang gỉ sét
rơi xuống một người chèo thuyền kayak
lập ngân sách cho một cây cầu (trong khi thực ra mô tả một đài tưởng niệm)
một đòn đánh trúng (lời thừa nhận đối phương nói đúng)
thừa nhận, nhượng bộ một điểm
tấm bảng tưởng niệm
có thể chuyển giao/thay thế bằng thứ khác

annotations.phrase: A clean replica would be a lie that photographs well
annotations.meaningVi: Một bản sao sạch sẽ sẽ là một lời nói dối nhưng lên ảnh đẹp
chính sự gỉ sét là đài tưởng niệm — luận điểm cốt lõi của nhà sử học
một lời nói dối nhưng lên ảnh đẹp — chỉ trích bản sao sạch sẽ
sự đổ nát, hư hỏng do thời gian
tấm séc khống — ẩn dụ cho quyền chi tiêu không giới hạn
chịu tải theo nghĩa khác — chơi chữ: cầu chịu tải vật lý, còn câu chuyện cộng đồng cũng 'chịu tải' tinh thần
gia cố, làm ổn định nó
một vật thể tĩnh (không dùng để đi qua)

một nửa cuộc tranh luận này tan biến (khi cả hai ngừng giả vờ nó là cây cầu)

grammarNotes.title: Fronting and emphatic 'it'-clefts in argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để nhấn mạnh điều cốt lõi, người nói C1 thường đảo trật tự thông tin hoặc dùng câu chẻ với 'it'. Nhà sử học nói: 'The rust isn't a flaw in the monument; the rust is the monument.' — cấu trúc lặp đối xứng (the rust ... the rust) làm bật nghịch lý. Kỹ sư dùng câu chẻ phủ định: 'What I can't do is certify a romance.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Emphasis is built by reordering and by 'what'-clefts. The historian's parallel inversion — 'The rust isn't a flaw in the monument; the rust is the monument' — repeats the subject to flip an expected judgement. The engineer answers with a cleft: 'What I can't do is certify a romance', front-loading the limit before naming it. Both devices push the key idea to a stressed position.

translationVi:
Engineer: Để tôi nói thẳng trước khi chúng ta trở nên ủy mị. Cây cầu đi bộ Pontfaen đang hỏng. Các thanh chịu kéo bằng sắt rèn đã mất có lẽ một phần ba tiết diện do ăn mòn, và hồ sơ của ông chủ xưởng sắt nguyên bản — mà tôi đã đọc — chưa bao giờ lường trước tải trọng nó đang chịu bây giờ. Việc của tôi là chứng nhận xem nó có an toàn hay không. Nó không an toàn. Dù nó có ý nghĩa gì với mọi người, một công trình không thể chịu nổi trọng lượng của chính nó thì không phải là tài sản di sản. Nó là một gánh nặng pháp lý kèm theo cảnh đẹp.
Historian: Và tôi sẽ không giả vờ rằng sự ăn mòn là tưởng tượng. Anh là kỹ sư; tôi nhường anh phần kim loại. Nhưng tôi muốn anh để ý rằng anh đã lén đưa vào một định nghĩa. Anh nói nó không phải tài sản di sản vì nó không chịu được tải. Điều đó giả định rằng tài sản di sản là thứ mà ta đi qua. Giá trị của cây cầu chưa bao giờ nằm ở việc đưa người từ bờ này sang bờ kia. Có một cây cầu đường hoàn toàn tốt cách hai trăm mét về hạ lưu và đã có từ lâu rồi.
Engineer: Vậy thì, chính xác là, nó dùng để làm gì? Tôi hỏi thật lòng. Nếu không ai cần đi qua nó, và nó nguy hiểm, thì điều tử tế nhất là một cuộc phá dỡ có kiểm soát trước khi dòng sông tự làm việc đó cho chúng ta, một cách bừa bộn, trong một trận lũ.
Historian: Nó dùng cho ký ức, dù tôi biết điều đó nghe yếu ớt thế nào với người đang cầm bản báo cáo ăn mòn. Pontfaen là cây cầu đi bộ bằng sắt đầu tiên được đúc bởi xưởng đúc Tregaron, và xưởng đúc đó đã thuê ba thế hệ của thung lũng này trước khi nó đóng cửa. Cây cầu là thứ vật chất cuối cùng họ làm ra mà vẫn còn đứng vững. Kéo nó xuống thì xưởng đúc trở thành một đoạn văn trong cuốn sách không ai mở ra. Để nó đứng — dù có rào lại, dù không thể đi qua — thì nó vẫn là một sự thật hiện hữu trong cảnh quan mà người ta có thể dắt một đứa trẻ tới và kể cho nó nghe.
Engineer: Tôi thấy điều đó thật sự cảm động, và tôi không mỉa mai. Nhưng vấn đề nằm ở chỗ này. 'Rào lại và không thể đi qua' không hề miễn phí. Có người phải trả tiền để kiểm tra nó hằng năm, để giữ công chúng tránh xa mặt cầu gỉ sét, để bảo hiểm cho cái ngày một mảnh của nó rơi trúng một người chèo kayak. Anh đang mô tả một đài tưởng niệm mà lại lập ngân sách cho một cây cầu. Nếu thung lũng muốn một đài tưởng niệm xưởng đúc, tôi có thể nghĩ ra cả tá cái rẻ hơn mà không cần treo hai tấn sắt đang hỏng trên dòng nước chảy.
Historian: Đó là một đòn trúng, và tôi sẽ nhượng bộ một phần. Một tấm bảng thì rẻ hơn. Một bản sao trong bãi đỗ xe bảo tàng thì rẻ hơn. Nhưng anh đang giả định rằng ý nghĩa có thể chuyển giao — rằng một tấm bảng mang được điều mà cây cầu mang. Nó không mang được. Cây cầu thuyết phục vì nó là vật thật, ở đúng nơi thật, chịu đúng thời tiết thật đã gặm nhấm nó suốt một thế kỷ. Sự gỉ sét không phải là một khiếm khuyết của đài tưởng niệm; chính sự gỉ sét là đài tưởng niệm. Một bản sao sạch sẽ sẽ là một lời nói dối nhưng lên ảnh đẹp.
Engineer: Vậy là anh muốn chúng ta tiêu tiền công để bảo tồn một mối nguy chính vì nó đang mục nát một cách đích thực. Anh thấy cái khó rồi đấy. Có một phiên bản của lập luận anh biện minh cho việc bảo tồn bất cứ thứ gì cũ, với bất kỳ giá nào, trên cơ sở rằng chính sự đổ nát của nó là có ý nghĩa. Đến một lúc nào đó một cộng đồng phải quyết định nó có thể giữ được những gì, và 'nó là vật thật' không thể là một tấm séc khống.
Historian: Không, không thể, và tôi sẽ không viết cho anh một tấm séc như thế. Tôi không biện hộ cho mọi thứ cũ. Tôi biện hộ cho vài thứ chịu tải theo nghĩa khác — những thứ mà câu chuyện của một cộng đồng thực sự tựa vào. Phép thử không phải là tuổi tác. Mà là liệu việc dỡ bỏ vật thể có xóa đi điều gì đó mà không gì khác có thể giữ được hay không. Theo phép thử đó, hầu hết những thứ cũ đều trượt, và đáng bị dỡ bỏ. Tôi chỉ không nghĩ cái này trượt.
Engineer: Vậy thì có lẽ chúng ta gần nhau hơn cái tiêu đề gợi ý. Tôi có lẽ có thể gia cố nó — không phải để đi qua, mà như một vật thể tĩnh — với một phần nhỏ chi phí phục chế toàn bộ, nếu hội đồng chấp nhận rằng nó sẽ không bao giờ mở lại. Cái tôi không thể làm là chứng nhận cho một mối lãng mạn. Hãy đưa cho tôi một đài tưởng niệm để thiết kế và tôi sẽ thiết kế nó một cách trung thực. Chỉ đừng yêu cầu tôi gọi nó là một cây cầu, bởi vì khoảnh khắc cả hai ta ngừng giả vờ rằng nó là một cây cầu, một nửa cuộc tranh luận này tan biến.
Historian: Tôi có thể chấp nhận điều đó. Có lẽ đó là sự nhượng bộ thật sự ở phía tôi — rằng cứu nó nghĩa là thừa nhận nó đã thôi là cái nó từng là. Chúng ta giữ nó bằng cách để nó trở thành thứ khác: không phải một lối qua sông, mà một lối quay về với những người đã xây nó. Tôi sẽ ký vào đó. Một cách miễn cưỡng, vì tôi thích tưởng tượng có ai đó có thể đi qua nó lần nữa. Nhưng tôi sẽ ký.

questions.prompt: What is the engineer's professional assessment of the Pontfaen footbridge?
options: It is sound but needs minor repairs
It is failing and cannot safely hold its own weight
It is safe for pedestrians but not for vehicles
It needs only a fresh coat of protective paint
questions.explanation: 'a structure that cannot hold its own weight is not a heritage asset. It is a liability with a nice view.'
questions.hint: The engineer's first speech states his verdict.

questions.prompt: What does the historian accuse the engineer of having 'smuggled in'?
options: A definition that assumes a heritage asset must be something you walk across
An unauthorised plan to demolish the bridge
A personal grudge against the foundry
questions.explanation: 'you've already smuggled in a definition ... That assumes a heritage asset is something you walk across.'
questions.hint: The historian's first reply challenges a hidden assumption.

questions.prompt: According to the historian, what is the bridge actually 'for'?
options: Crossing the river when the road bridge is closed
Generating tourist revenue for the valley
Memory — preserving the last standing object made by the Tregaron foundry
questions.explanation: 'It's for memory ... The bridge is the last physical thing they made that's still standing.'
questions.hint: The historian answers the engineer's 'what is it for?' question directly.

questions.prompt: What does the engineer mean by saying the historian is 'describing a monument and budgeting for a bridge'?
options: That monuments are always more expensive than bridges
That keeping the bridge fenced off still carries the ongoing costs of a working bridge despite no longer functioning as one
That the historian has confused two different bridges
That the council has allocated the wrong budget line
questions.explanation: 'Someone pays to inspect it annually, to keep the public from the rusting deck, to insure against the day a bit of it lands on a kayaker.'
questions.hint: The engineer lists the hidden ongoing costs of a 'fenced off' bridge.

questions.prompt: Why does the historian argue that a clean replica would be 'a lie that photographs well'?
options: Because replicas are usually made of cheaper materials
Because the meaning depends on the bridge being the actual thing, weathered in the actual place — the rust IS the monument
Because photographs of replicas are easily faked
Because museums profit unfairly from replicas
questions.explanation: 'The bridge convinces because it is the actual thing ... The rust isn't a flaw in the monument; the rust is the monument.'
questions.hint: The historian explains why meaning is not 'transferable' to a replica.

questions.prompt: What weakness does the engineer identify in the historian's 'it's the real thing' argument?
options: That real things are always more dangerous than replicas
That it could justify preserving anything old, at any cost, making 'it's the real thing' a blank cheque
That historians cannot be trusted to assess authenticity
That the public does not care about authenticity
questions.explanation: 'There's a version of your argument that justifies preserving anything old, at any cost ... 'it's the real thing' can't be a blank cheque.'
questions.hint: The engineer warns the argument 'proves too much'.

questions.prompt: How does the historian refine his position in response to the 'blank cheque' objection?
options: He insists that all old objects deserve preservation
He proposes a test: whether removing the object would erase something nothing else can hold
He says only the council can decide
questions.explanation: 'The test isn't age. It's whether removing the object would erase something that nothing else can hold.'
questions.hint: The historian offers a specific test to limit his own claim.

questions.prompt: What compromise does the engineer propose near the end?
options: Full restoration so the bridge can reopen to pedestrians
Immediate demolition followed by a plaque
Stabilising it as a static object that will never reopen, for a fraction of full restoration
Selling the iron to the museum
questions.explanation: 'I could probably stabilise it — not for crossing, but as a static object — for a fraction of full restoration ... if the council accepts it will never reopen.'
questions.hint: The engineer's penultimate speech proposes a middle path.

questions.prompt: What does the engineer mean by 'I can't certify a romance'?
options: He cannot approve a wedding on the bridge
He cannot sign off on safety based on sentiment; he can only engineer a clearly defined object honestly
He dislikes romantic descriptions of engineering
He refuses to work with historians
questions.explanation: 'Give me a monument to engineer and I'll engineer it honestly ... don't ask me to call it a bridge.' He needs an honest definition, not sentiment, to do his job.
questions.hint: The engineer distinguishes between engineering a 'monument' and a 'bridge'.

questions.prompt: What is 'the real concession' the historian makes at the end?
options: That the bridge should be demolished after all
That saving the bridge means admitting it has stopped being what it was — a way across the river
That the engineer's corrosion report was exaggerated
questions.explanation: 'saving it means admitting it has stopped being what it was. We keep it by letting it become something else.'
questions.hint: The historian names his own concession in his final speech.

questions.prompt: Which of the following does the engineer NOT claim?
options: That the bridge is unsafe and cannot bear load
That keeping the bridge fenced off still costs money
That the historian's emotional argument is contemptible and worthless
That preserving anything old at any cost is unaffordable
questions.explanation: The engineer explicitly says 'I find that genuinely moving, and I'm not being sarcastic.' He respects the emotional argument; he disputes only its costing.
questions.hint: Look at how the engineer responds to the 'memory' argument.

questions.prompt: The phrase 'load-bearing in the other sense' (historian) is a play on words. What does the second sense mean?
options: Objects on which a community's story or identity rests
questions.explanation: 'the ones a community's story actually rests on' — a figurative load: the cultural weight the object carries, not a physical one.
questions.hint: The historian extends the engineering term to culture.

questions.prompt: Which of these two paraphrases is closer to the position the two reach by the end?
options: The bridge should be preserved exactly as a functioning bridge, because authenticity demands it
The bridge can be kept as a stabilised monument, but only if both sides admit it is no longer a usable bridge
The bridge should be demolished because sentiment cannot justify the cost
The bridge should be replaced with a clean replica in the museum car park
questions.explanation: The engineer offers to stabilise it 'as a static object' and the historian agrees, conceding 'it has stopped being what it was' — agreement conditioned on redefining it as a monument.
questions.hint: Both final speeches converge on the same conditional resolution.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the tone of the debate as a whole?
options: Hostile and unresolved, with neither side conceding anything
Respectful and exploratory, each conceding something true to the other
One-sided, with the engineer dominating throughout
Comic, treating the dispute as trivial
questions.explanation: Each grants the other genuine ground ('a fair hit, and I'll concede part of it'; 'I find that genuinely moving'), narrowing toward a shared position.
questions.hint: Notice the repeated language of concession on both sides.

questions.prompt: The word 'dilapidation' (engineer's speech) most nearly means:
options: a state of decay or disrepair
questions.explanation: 'its very dilapidation is meaningful' — the run-down, decaying condition of the structure.
questions.hint: The engineer is paraphrasing the historian's 'the rust is the monument' idea.

questions.prompt: Why does the historian sign the compromise 'reluctantly'?
options: Because he distrusts the engineer's costings
Because he had liked imagining someone might cross the bridge again
Because the council might still demolish it
Because he wanted a clean replica instead
questions.explanation: 'Reluctantly, because I liked imagining someone might cross it again. But I'd sign it.'
questions.hint: The historian's final words explain his reluctance.

cloze.template:
Engineer: Let me be {{b1}} before we get sentimental. The Pontfaen footbridge is failing. The wrought-iron tension members have lost perhaps a third of their section to {{b2}}, and the original ironmaster's records — which I've read — never anticipated the loads it now carries. My job is to {{b3}} whether it is safe. It is not. Whatever it means to people, a structure that cannot hold its own weight is not a heritage asset. It is a {{b4}} with a nice view.
Historian: And I won't pretend the corrosion is imaginary. You're the engineer; I {{b5}} to you on the metal. But I'd ask you to notice that you've already {{b6}} in a definition. You said it's not a heritage asset because it can't carry load. That assumes a heritage asset is something you walk across. The bridge's value was never in getting people from one bank to the other.
Engineer: Then what, precisely, is it for? I'm genuinely asking. If nobody needs to cross it, and it's dangerous, the kindest thing is a controlled {{b7}} before the river does it for us, messily, in a flood.
Historian: It's for memory, though I know how soft that sounds to someone holding a corrosion report. Pontfaen was the first iron footbridge cast by the Tregaron {{b8}}, and that foundry employed three generations of this valley before it closed. The bridge is the last physical thing they made that's still standing. Pull it down and the foundry becomes a paragraph in a book nobody opens.
Engineer: I find that genuinely moving, and I'm not being sarcastic. But here's the {{b9}}. 'Fenced off and uncrossable' is not free. Someone pays to inspect it annually, to keep the public from the rusting deck, to insure against the day a bit of it lands on a kayaker. You're describing a monument and {{b10}} for a bridge.
Historian: That's a fair hit, and I'll {{b11}} part of it. A plaque is cheaper. A replica in the museum car park is cheaper. But you're assuming the meaning is {{b12}} — that a plaque carries what the bridge carries. It doesn't. The bridge convinces because it is the actual thing, in the actual place, bearing the actual weather that's been eating it for a century. The rust isn't a flaw in the monument; the rust is the monument. A clean replica would be a lie that photographs well.
Engineer: So you'd have us spend public money preserving a hazard precisely because it's decaying authentically. There's a version of your argument that justifies preserving anything old, at any cost, on the grounds that its very {{b13}} is meaningful. At some point a community has to decide what it can afford to keep, and 'it's the real thing' can't be a blank cheque.
Historian: No, it can't, and I wouldn't write you one. I'm not arguing for everything old. I'm arguing for the few things that are {{b14}} in the other sense — the ones a community's story actually rests on. The test isn't age. It's whether removing the object would erase something that nothing else can hold.
Engineer: Then we may be closer than the headline suggests. I could probably {{b15}} it — not for crossing, but as a static object — for a fraction of full restoration, if the council accepts it will never reopen.
Historian: I can live with that. We keep it by letting it become something else: not a way across the river, but a way back to the people who built it.

blanks.explanation: 'Let me be blunt before we get sentimental' — 'blunt' (saying hard truths without softening) is the sharpest fit and contrasts with 'sentimental'.
'lost perhaps a third of their section to corrosion' — the technical term for the chemical degradation of metal; 'rust' is informal, 'erosion' is for soil/rock.
'My job is to certify whether it is safe' — the formal verb for an engineer issuing an official safety attestation.
'It is a liability with a nice view' — 'liability' carries the legal/financial sense of something one is answerable for, sharpening the irony.
'I defer to you on the metal' — to defer to someone is to respectfully accept their expert authority; the precise idiom.
'you've already smuggled in a definition' — the loaded verb implying a definition was introduced without acknowledgement; the fixed phrase is 'smuggle in'.
'a controlled demolition' — the standard collocation for a planned, safe pulling-down of a structure.
'cast by the Tregaron foundry' — a foundry is specifically where metal is cast; the word recurs and 'cast' demands it.
'But here's the rub' — the fixed idiom (from Hamlet) meaning 'here is the difficulty'; the others don't take 'here's the ___'.
'describing a monument and budgeting for a bridge' — 'budgeting for' sets up the parallel contrast the engineer is drawing.
'I'll concede part of it' — to concede is to yield a point in argument; the debate-register verb that pairs with 'a fair hit'.
'you're assuming the meaning is transferable' — that meaning can be carried over to another object; 'transferable' is the abstract-quality term needed.
'its very dilapidation is meaningful' — the formal noun for falling into disrepair, echoing 'the rust is the monument'.
'the few things that are load-bearing in the other sense' — deliberately reuses the engineering term as a metaphor for cultural weight.
'I could probably stabilise it ... as a static object' — 'stabilise' specifically means make structurally secure without restoring full function, exactly the compromise.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The historian proposes a test for what deserves saving: not age, but whether removing the object would 'erase something that nothing else can hold.' Apply this test to something contested in your own community — a building, a custom, an institution. Does it pass? And is the historian right that authenticity ('the rust is the monument') can never be carried by a replica, or are there things a faithful copy can preserve just as well?
